text: Given the production possibilities curve above, which of the following represents a movement from efficiency to inefficiency?

options.A: Point X to point W
options.B: Point X to point Z
options.C: Point Y to point Z
options.D: Point W to point Z
options.E: Point V to point Y

text: Based on a comparison of points X, Y, and Z, the opportunity cost of an additional consumer good is

options.A: highest at point X
options.B: highest at point Y
options.C: highest at point Z
options.D: lowest at point Y

text: The table above shows the quantity of gasoline supplied and demanded at various prices in a country. If the government sets a price floor of $2.75 on a gallon of gasoline, what is the price per gallon?

Last year 17 million tons of beans were sold for $300 per ton. This year 17 million tons of beans were sold for $285 per ton. Which of the follow­ ing changes in demand and supply could have caused this outcome? Demand Supply

options.A: Increase Increase
options.B: Increase Decrease
options.C: Decrease Increase
options.D: Decrease Decrease
options.E: Increase No change any part of this page is illegal. M U sub s equals M U sub h equals P sub s equals P sub h P  sub s less than P sub h P  sub s greater than P sub h

text: Which of the following will most likely happen in the market for good X if the price of good X decreases?

options.A: The supply of good X will decrease.
options.B: The demand for good X will increase.
options.C: The quantity demanded for good X will increase.
options.D: The demand will decrease and the supply will increase.
options.E: The quantity supplied for good X will increase.

text: Let U_s be the marginal utility of a sandwich, MU_h be the marginal utility of a hot dog, P_s be the price of a sandwich, and P_h be the price of a hot dog. When the price of the goods is zero, Pat eats a sandwich. When Pat has to pay, she eats a hot dog. When Pat has to pay for both, she chooses the combination of sandwiches and hot dogs that maximizes her utility. Which of the following best explains Pat's choices?

options.A: A sandwich is a normal good, and a hot dog is an inferior good.
options.B: A sandwich is an inferior good, and a hot dog is a normal good.
options.C: The marginal utility per dollar spent on sandwiches is greater than the marginal utility per dollar spent on hot dogs.
options.D: The marginal utility per dollar spent on hot dogs is greater than the marginal utility per dollar spent on sandwiches.
options.E: The marginal utility of sandwiches is greater than the marginal utility of hot dogs.

text: Assume that labor is the only variable input. If a firm’s short-run marginal cost is increasing as output rises, which of the following must be true?

options.A: Average product of labor is constant.
options.B: Average product of labor is increasing.
options.C: Marginal product of labor is greater than average product of labor.
options.D: Marginal product of labor is decreasing.
options.E: Total product of labor is decreasing.

text: Which of the following is most likely to occur if a single-price monopolist is replaced by a perfectly competitive market?

options.A: Prices will increase.
options.B: The deadweight loss will decrease.
options.C: Profits will increase.
options.D: Output will decrease.
options.E: The firm’s cost curves will shift upward.

text: Suppose that price in a perfectly competitive industry decreases and it is now below minimum average total cost but remains above minimum average variable cost. Which of the following will occur in the short run?

options.A: New firms will enter the industry.
options.B: Firms will increase output so that marginal revenue equals the new price.
options.C: Firms will produce the output at which average total cost is at a minimum.
options.D: Firms will produce the output at which marginal cost equals the new price.
options.E: Firms will not produce at all, since they will be unable to cover all their costs.

text: Which of the following is true for both a monopolistically competitive firm and a perfectly competitive firm in long-run equilibrium?

options.A: Marginal cost is greater than marginal revenue.
options.B: Price is greater than marginal cost.
options.C: Price is equal to average total cost.
options.D: Price is equal to marginal cost.
options.E: Marginal revenue is equal to average revenue. any part of this page is illegal.

text: Which of the following will most likely lead to zero economic profits?

options.A: Price floors
options.B: Price discrimination
options.C: External costs
options.D: Free entry and exit of firms
options.E: External benefits

text: A perfectly competitive firm hires three workers in a perfectly competitive labor market. The marginal products of the three workers are shown in the table below. Worker Marginal Product 1 50 2 30 3 20 Which of the following will be true?

options.A: Each worker will receive a wage based on the marginal product of the last worker hired.
options.B: Each worker will receive a wage based on the marginal product of the first worker hired.
options.C: Each worker will receive a wage based on the average of the marginal products of the workers.
options.D: Worker 1 will receive the highest wage.
options.E: Worker 3 will receive the highest wage.

text: Assume a perfectly competitive labor market. Which of the following correctly describes the individual firm’s demand curve for labor and the market demand curve for labor? Firm’s Demand Curve for Labor Market Demand Curve for Labor

options.A: Downward sloping
options.B: Downward sloping
options.C: Horizontal
options.D: Horizontal
options.E: Horizontal Downward sloping Horizontal Downward sloping Horizontal Upward sloping

text: When consumption of a good generates a positive externality, which of the following must be true at the market equilibrium?

options.A: Marginal social benefit is less than marginal private cost.
options.B: Marginal social benefit is greater than marginal private benefit.
options.C: Marginal social cost is greater than marginal social benefit.
options.D: Marginal social cost is less than marginal private benefit.
options.E: Marginal social cost is equal to marginal external benefit. any part of this page is illegal.

text: National defense is an example of a public good because

options.A: it requires tax revenues to fund any production
options.B: one person's use of it will decrease another person's ability to use it
options.C: it is nonexcludable and nonrival
options.D: the private market typically produces the socially efficient quantity
options.E: it is both excludable and rival

text: The amount of product Z that must be forgone in order to obtain some amount of product Y is called

options.A: factor payments
options.B: opportunity cost
options.C: marginal product
options.D: income effect
options.E: substitution effect

text: Which of the following is necessary in a well-functioning capitalist economy but not in a command economy?

options.A: Centralized decision making
options.B: Scarcity of resources
options.C: Monopolies
options.D: Protection of property rights
options.E: Positive externalities

text: If an unusually cold summer destroyed a large portion of the bee population, the equilibrium price and quantity of honey produced by bees will most likely change in which of the following ways? Price Quantity

options.A: have no effect on the total revenue of insulin producers
options.B: increase the total revenue of insulin producers
options.C: decrease the total revenue of insulin producers
options.D: decrease the total spending on insulin by consumers
options.E: cause the demand for insulin to be less elastic

text: If the demand for insulin is price inelastic, a 5 percent increase in the price of insulin will

options.A: it requires tax revenues to fund any production
options.B: one person’s use of it will decrease another person’s ability to use it
options.C: it is nonexcludable and nonrival
options.D: the private market typically produces the socially efficient level of output
options.E: the public is protected from invasion

text: Which of the following would shift the short-run supply curve for strawberries?

options.A: A decrease in the consumption of strawberries
options.B: An increase in the price of strawberries
options.C: A strike by all farmworkers
options.D: An increase in household incomes
options.E: An announcement of a study that shows the health benefits of eating strawberries

text: Assume that good X is a normal good. Which of the following helps to explain why a decrease in the price of good X increases the quantity demanded of good X?

options.A: Good X becomes relatively less expensive than its substitutes, so consumers buy more of good X and fewer of the substitutes.
options.B: The marginal utility of consuming good X increases as more of good X is consumed.
options.C: The lower price of good X decreases the marginal utility per dollar; therefore, consumers buy more of good X.
options.D: The demand curve for good X shifts to the left.
options.E: The supply curve for good X shifts to the right. any part of this page is illegal.

text: Which of the following conditions is necessary for productive efficiency?

options.A: The firm is producing at the point where the marginal product of labor is greater than the average product of labor.
options.B: The firm is producing a given level of output with a least-cost combination of inputs.
options.C: Consumer surplus is minimized.
options.D: There are external costs.
options.E: There is no government regulation.

text: Which of the following indicates that a firm is experiencing economies of scale?

options.A: The firm’s long-run supply curve is horizontal.
options.B: The firm’s long-run marginal cost increases as output increases.
options.C: The firm’s long-run average total cost decreases as output increases.
options.D: The firm’s long-run total cost decreases as output increases.
options.E: The firm’s total revenues increase as output sold increases.

text: All of the following characterize both perfectly competitive and monopolistically competitive markets EXCEPT:

options.A: Price is equal to average revenue.
options.B: Individual firms produce output where marginal cost equals marginal revenue.
options.C: Firms can affect the selling price of their product.
options.D: The market has a large number of firms.
options.E: Firms can easily enter or exit the market.

text: Which of the following is true of a monopolistically competitive firm in long-run equilibrium?

options.A: Price exceeds marginal revenue, and the firm earns positive economic profits.
options.B: Price equals marginal revenue, and the firm earns positive economic profits.
options.C: Price equals marginal cost, and the firm earns zero economic profits.
options.D: Price exceeds marginal cost, and the firm earns zero economic profits.
options.E: Price exceeds marginal cost, and the firm earns positive economic profits.

text: A firm is producing the allocatively efficient level of output if

options.A: total revenue is equal to total cost
options.B: marginal revenue is equal to marginal cost
options.C: price is equal to average total cost
options.D: price is equal to marginal cost
options.E: price is equal to total cost

text: Assume that labor and capital are the only two inputs a perfectly competitive firm uses to produce wheat. The firm hires its inputs in perfectly competitive input markets. The unit price of labor is $8 and of capital is $20. When the firm employs the profit-maximizing combination of these two inputs, the marginal product of labor is 2 tons of wheat and of capital is 5 tons of wheat. The price of wheat per ton must be

options.E: $10 any part of this page is illegal.

text: A profit-maximizing firm will continue to hire workers until the marginal revenue product of labor is equal to the

options.A: marginal product of labor
options.B: marginal revenue product of capital
options.C: demand for labor
options.D: price of the good that labor is producing
options.E: marginal factor cost

text: If the production of a good generates a negative externality, which of the following is true at the private market equilibrium?

options.A: The private market equilibrium quantity is greater than the socially optimal quantity.
options.B: The private market equilibrium quantity is equal to the socially optimal quantity.
options.C: The marginal private cost is greater than the marginal social cost.
options.D: The marginal social benefit is greater than the marginal social cost.
options.E: The price of the product equals the marginal social cost.

text: Which of the following is most likely to make a country’s income distribution more equal?

options.A: Establishing a national sales tax on food and clothing
options.B: Establishing a progressive income tax system
options.C: Eliminating excise taxes on luxury goods
options.D: Proportionally decreasing the tax rate for all income groups
options.E: Reducing the subsidy to education

text: The table below shows the number of hours it takes for Kim to install a computer or to paint a garage and for Maria to install a computer or to paint a garage. Kim Maria Install computer 2 hours 4 hours Paint garage 10 hours 12 hours If tasks are assigned according to comparative advantage, which of the following is true?

options.A: Kim should perform both tasks.
options.B: Maria should perform both tasks.
options.C: Kim should install computers and Maria should paint garages.
options.D: Kim should paint garages and Maria should install computers.
options.E: Kim and Maria should both install computers. any part of this page is illegal. text: In the figure above, at which of the given points is demand most elastic?

options.A: X
options.B: Y
options.C: Z
options.D: The elasticity is the same for all points.
options.E: The relative elasticity cannot be determined with the given information.

text: Consumer surplus exists because of the

options.A: surplus of money that wealthy consumers hold
options.B: surplus in marginal product that firms get when they hire an additional worker
options.C: willingness of some consumers to pay a price higher than the market price for some units of a good
options.D: additional utility that households obtain when the prices of substitutes fall
options.E: increase in the availability of goods when income rises

text: Which of the following is a key assumption of consumer choice theory?

options.A: Consumers buy goods as long as implicit costs are greater than explicit costs.
options.B: Consumers maximize their utility.
options.C: As income rises, consumers buy more of all goods.
options.D: Marginal utility is always increasing.
options.E: As more inputs are used, consumers get less and less additional output.

text: Economic profit can be calculated as accounting profit minus which of the following?

options.A: Fixed costs
options.B: Implicit costs
options.C: Marginal costs
options.D: Explicit costs
options.E: Total costs any part of this page is illegal. Quantity (units) Total Cost 0 $30 1 40 2 47 3 51 4 59

text: The table above shows a firm’s total cost of producing various units of output. What is the average variable cost of producing three units?

options.E: Cannot be determined from the given information

text: Assume that the corn market is initially in long- run equilibrium at point R. What are the short-run and long-run prices of corn if more corn is used as a source of alternative energy? Short Run Long Run

options.A: P P sub one
options.B: P P sub two
options.C: P sub one P sub two
options.D: p sub two p sub one
options.E: p sub two P

text: The long-run industry supply curve for corn is

options.A: upward sloping
options.B: downward sloping
options.C: horizontal
options.D: downward sloping at first and then upward sloping
options.E: upward sloping at first and then downward sloping

text: Which of the following is necessarily true at a monopolist’s profit-maximizing level of output?

options.A: Marginal revenue is equal to marginal cost, but greater than price.
options.B: Marginal revenue is equal to marginal cost, but less than price.
options.C: Marginal revenue is equal to both marginal cost and price.
options.D: Average total cost is minimized.
options.E: Average variable cost is minimized.

text: Assume that the government imposes an effective minimum wage in a perfectly competitive labor market. What will happen to employment and total wage payments? Employment Total Wage Payments

options.A: Decrease Increase
options.B: Decrease Decrease
options.C: Decrease Indeterminate
options.D: Increase Increase
options.E: Increase Indeterminate

text: Suppose that people who work in the paint industry face a great risk of developing an incurable disease.

options.A: Shift right No shift
options.B: Shift left No shift
options.C: Shift left Shift left
options.D: Shift left Shift right
options.E: No shift Shift left any part of this page is illegal.

text: Assuming that all residents of the city of Smallville prefer to have streetlights installed citywide, which of the following best explains why individual residents do not install the optimal number of streetlights?

options.A: Streetlights generate negative externalities.
options.B: Streetlights are nonexcludable.
options.C: Installing streetlights results in diseconomies of scale.
options.D: The marginal cost of installing streetlights is zero.
options.E: The marginal benefit of installing streetlights is zero.

text: Antitrust policy is designed to

options.A: encourage businesses to produce public goods
options.B: establish trust between the government and the business community
options.C: prevent businesses from earning profits
options.D: maintain a competitive business environment
options.E: ensure that businesses earn only zero economic profits in the long run

text: Which of the following is true of an economy’s production possibilities curve?

options.A: It shows the combinations of any two resources that can be used to produce an efficient level of output.
options.B: It shows the alternative combinations of goods that can be produced by fully employing scarce resources.
options.C: It must be a straight line when all resources are fully employed.
options.D: It is bowed in (convex to the origin) because of changing levels of technology.
options.E: It is bowed out (concave to the origin) when marginal opportunity costs are constant.

text: If shirts and ties are complements and if the price of shirts increases due to an increase in the price of cotton, which of the following is most likely to occur in the market for ties in the short run?

options.A: The equilibrium price and quantity of ties will increase.
options.B: The equilibrium price and quantity of ties will decrease.
options.C: The equilibrium price of ties will increase and the equilibrium quantity will decrease.
options.D: The supply of ties will increase.
options.E: The demand for ties will increase.

text: Which of the following is true of the cross-price elasticity of demand?

options.A: It can indicate if a good is a necessity or a luxury.
options.B: It is greater than zero for two goods that are substitutes.
options.C: It is close to zero if the two goods are closely related.
options.D: It is always negative because demand curves are downward sloping.
options.E: It increases as income increases.

text: Oscar spent his entire income on only two goods: good X and good Y. At his current consumption of the two goods, the marginal utility of X is 8 and the marginal utility of Y is 2. If the price of X is $4.00 and the price of Y is $0.50, then to maximize his total utility, Oscar should have

options.A: bought more X and more Y
options.B: bought more X and less Y
options.C: bought less X and more Y
options.D: bought less X and less Y
options.E: maintained his current consumption any part of this page is illegal.

text: In the short run, a profit-maximizing firm, faced with U-shaped average cost curves, is producing a level of output at which the average total cost of production is minimized. At this level of output, which of the following is true for the firm?

options.A: Marginal cost equals average fixed cost.
options.B: Marginal cost equals average variable cost.
options.C: Marginal cost equals average total cost.
options.D: Profit per unit equals average total cost.
options.E: Profit per unit equals marginal cost.

text: Which of the following must be true for a firm that is a natural monopoly?

options.A: It can produce and supply its product to an entire market at a lower cost than could a number of smaller firms.
options.B: It produces at the minimum of its average total cost curve.
options.C: It has a patent on its product.
options.D: It will not be able to maximize profits unless subsidized by the government.
options.E: It is productively efficient for the government to break up the monopoly into smaller firms.

text: In the short run, if a firm produces the level of output at which marginal revenue is equal to marginal cost but price is less than average total cost, the firm will

options.A: always shut down production
options.B: expand output to lower its average fixed cost
options.C: continue to operate if price is greater than its average variable cost
options.D: decrease output until price equals its average total cost
options.E: increase output to increase revenue

text: Assume that a firm uses only one variable input. If a firm is experiencing diminishing returns, which of the following is true as more of the variable input is used?

options.A: Marginal cost will decrease at a constant rate.
options.B: Marginal cost will decrease at a diminishing rate.
options.C: Marginal cost will increase.
options.D: Marginal product will increase at a constant rate.
options.E: Marginal product will increase at a diminishing rate.

text: Assuming a linear downward-sloping demand curve, as a monopoly firm sells additional units of output, its marginal revenue will

options.A: increase at an increasing rate
options.B: increase at first, then decrease
options.C: decrease at first, then increase
options.D: decrease continuously
options.E: remain constant

text: Collusion, price leadership, and price wars are usually observed in which of the following market structures?

options.A: Perfect competition
options.B: Monopolistic competition
options.C: Oligopoly
options.D: Monopoly
options.E: Natural monopoly any part of this page is illegal. P2

If the firm is choosing the least-cost combination of labor and capital, the firm's marginal product of labor and the price of capital must be equal to which of the following? Marginal Product of Labor Price of Capital

The figure shows a graph for a monopsonistic labor market with a horizontal axis labeled Number of Workers and a vertical axis labeled Wage. Two quantities appear on the horizontal axis and are labeled, from left to right, L sub one and L sub 2. Four prices appear on the vertical axis and are labeled, from bottom to top, W sub one, W sub 2, W sub 3, and W sub 4. There are two upward sloping lines and one downward sloping line on the graph. The leftmost upward sloping line is labeled Marginal Factor Cost, and begins to the right of the vertical axis and below a wage of W sub one, moves up and to the right, intersects the downward sloping line at L sub one and W sub 3, continues on, passing through the coordinates for L sub 2 and W sub 4. The rightmost upward sloping line is labeled Supply, and begins slightly below the start of the Marginal Factor Cost line, moves up and to the right, increasing in distance from the Marginal Factor Cost line as it moves up, and intersects the downward sloping line at L sub 2 and W sub 2, continues on, and ends to right of and below the Marginal Factor Cost line. The downward sloping line is labeled Marginal Revenue Product, and begins to the right of the vertical axis and above wage W sub 4 , moves down and to the right, intersects the marginal Factor Cost line at L sub one and W sub 3, then intersects the Supply line at L sub 2 and W sub 2, and continues on. The upward and downward sloping lines do not cross the horizontal or vertical axis.
